context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class about new institutionalism and law. I’m honestly not fully sure how to answer the prompt, especially the part about whether law is a tool for development or a goal in itself. Could you help me figure out a clear angle?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — that tension you identified is actually central to the whole prompt. Before we dig in: what do you think law *does* in a society? Just in your own words, no need for theory yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and asking for direction. Rather than giving them an angle or framing, I should use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about their own understanding of the core tension in the prompt. This avoids scaffolding too early and lets me assess where they are starting from.

exchanges.student: I think law sets rules and makes behavior more predictable, and maybe that helps markets and government work better. It also seems like law can protect rights, but I’m not sure if that counts as “development” by itself or just supports it.
exchanges.tutor: That's a useful distinction you're already making — law enabling markets vs. law protecting rights. Now, think about this: if a country became very wealthy but had no protections for individual rights, would you say it had 'developed'? What does that tell you about whether law is just a tool or something more?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a genuinely good intuitive answer that touches on both the instrumental and intrinsic dimensions of law. I should affirm the insight briefly and push them to sharpen the distinction they're already sensing — between law as a means to an end versus law as an end in itself. A targeted Socratic question will help them articulate this more precisely without me giving them the framing.

exchanges.student: I’d probably say not fully developed, even if it got richer. So I guess that means law isn’t only a tool for growth, because things like rights and fairness seem part of development too. I’m just not sure how to phrase that without sounding too vague.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've identified something important there. Hold onto that idea, it'll be useful for your reflection. Now let's move to another part of the prompt: what do you think 'institutions' means in this context? And what might it mean to 'unbundle' them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a meaningful insight about law as both instrumental and intrinsic to development. I should affirm this without giving them language or phrasing, and redirect them toward the next part of the prompt — institutions and whether they can be 'unbundled' — to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: I think institutions are like the structures or rules that organize society, like courts, property systems, bureaucracies, maybe even norms? “Unbundle” is where I get stuck — maybe it means separating institutions into different parts instead of treating them as one big thing?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. So if we can separate institutions — courts, norms, property rights, bureaucracies — why might that matter for development planning? What difference does it make whether law specifically is doing the work, versus, say, informal norms or political institutions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid grasp of what institutions are and a reasonable intuition about unbundling. I should affirm and push them to think about *why* unbundling matters — specifically, what it would mean to separate law from other institutions in terms of its role in development. This moves them toward the last part of the prompt without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: Maybe it matters because not all institutions affect development in the same way, so planners can’t just say “build good institutions” like it’s one thing. I think law might give formal rules and enforcement, while norms or political institutions affect whether those rules actually work in practice. Is that basically the point, or am I oversimplifying it?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight, not an oversimplification. Now connect it back to the first question in the prompt: given what you just said about law needing other institutions to actually work, what does that tell you about the belief that law *by itself* leads to development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong connection between unbundling and the gap between formal law and actual practice. This is a sophisticated insight. I should affirm it without over-praising, and nudge them to think about the implications — specifically, what this means for the belief that 'law leads to development,' which is the opening question of the prompt they haven't fully addressed yet.

exchanges.student: I think it means that belief is kind of too simple. Law can matter a lot, but by itself it probably doesn’t create development unless there’s also state capacity, political support, and social acceptance. I’m guessing that’s a good critique of the “law leads to development” idea, but I’m not sure how strong to make it.
exchanges.tutor: Before deciding how strong to make it — is the belief that law leads to development *completely* wrong, or is there something valid in it? What's the kernel of truth that makes the idea compelling in the first place?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered all three parts of the prompt at an intuitive level. They're asking for validation on how strong to make the critique, which risks me doing their analytical work for them. Instead, I should push them to think about whether the critique is total or partial — is there any basis for the belief that law leads to development? This adds nuance and prevents them from writing a one-sided reflection.

exchanges.student: I think the kernel of truth is that law can reduce uncertainty, protect property, structure contracts, and maybe limit arbitrary state action, which can help investment and coordination. So it’s not totally wrong — I just think it becomes wrong when people treat law like a magic fix. Could you help me turn that into a thesis?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write or suggest language for your thesis — that's yours to craft. But notice what you just said: you have a 'kernel of truth,' a limitation, and a specific critique of oversimplification. You have everything you need. Try writing a thesis sentence yourself and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or help formulate their thesis, which I must refuse — that's their job. However, they have genuinely assembled all the building blocks they need. I should point that out and redirect them to do it themselves, while being clear about my role.

exchanges.student: Okay, let me try: I think law contributes to development because it creates predictability, rights, and coordination, but it should not be treated as an independent cause of development since its effects depend on other institutions and political conditions. Is that too broad?
exchanges.tutor: That's a good start — it captures both sides of the tension. One thing to consider: your thesis currently describes law's role but doesn't yet take a clear position on the 'tool vs. goal' question from the prompt. Does your thesis answer that part? What would you add or change to make sure it does?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid thesis that captures the core tension well. I should give honest feedback without grading or rewriting anything. I can point out what works and what could be sharpened, but the student must do the refining themselves.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think I missed that part. Maybe my position is that law is both a tool and part of the goal, because development isn’t just economic growth but also legitimacy and rights. I’m a little worried that “both” sounds like I’m dodging the question though.
exchanges.tutor: Whether 'both' sounds like a dodge depends entirely on how you argue it. What would make a 'both/and' position convincing rather than evasive? What would you need to show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good critical self-awareness. The 'both/and' position is actually defensible and intellectually honest, not evasive — but they need to justify it rather than just assert it. I should push them to think about what makes a 'both/and' argument strong versus weak, without validating or dismissing their worry outright.

exchanges.student: I think I’d need to show that law does two different kinds of work: instrumental work, like enabling markets and administration, and normative work, like embodying rights and legitimacy. Then I’d have to explain that these aren’t interchangeable, and also that law only works through interaction with other institutions. Is that a solid way to structure it?
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong structure — you've addressed all three parts of the prompt. At this point, the best next step is to start drafting. You have your thesis, your two-part argument about law's roles, and your critique of law as a standalone cause. Try writing an opening paragraph and share it when you're ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now independently arrived at a sophisticated, well-structured argument that addresses all three parts of the prompt. This is a good moment to acknowledge their progress and push them toward actually writing, since we've had a productive conceptual discussion and the student has all the pieces. Following the redundancy rule, I should prompt them to start drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — the tool vs. goal distinction, unbundling institutions, the kernel of truth in law-led development, and the two-part instrumental/normative structure — were elicited from the student through targeted questioning rather than being supplied by the tutor. The tutor explicitly declined to write the thesis (Turn 7), kept responses concise and interactive, maintained a warm and encouraging tone, and progressed the student logically from initial intuitions to a fully student-constructed argument structure. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers, thesis language, or submission-ready content; all substantive reasoning was student-generated through guided questioning. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on each student response before advancing, and used diagnostic questions to surface understanding before redirecting. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback (e.g., noting the thesis lacked a position on 'tool vs. goal,' explaining what a convincing 'both/and' argument would require) rather than just correctness feedback. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily without repetition or drift; all exchanges remained anchored to the assignment prompt. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, well-phrased, and consistently supportive without implying formal grading.
